intro: Rama (the Greek and Hebrew form of Ramah, meaning height) is used in Matthew 2:18 in the quotation from Jeremiah 31:15: A voice was heard in Rama, lamentation and bitter weeping — Rachel weeping for her children. Matthew applies this text to the massacre of the infants in Bethlehem by Herod. Ramah was a town in Benjamin, approximately five miles north of Jerusalem, near the border with Ephraim, where the road north passed through the hill country. It became associated with Rachel's lament because tradition held that her tomb was nearby, and because the Benjaminite exiles were assembled there before deportation to Babylon (Jeremiah 40:1) — a historical fulfillment of Jeremiah's weeping Rachel image that Matthew then extends forward to Herod's slaughter.
